02-05.summary: A young noblewoman of third-century Catania whom the Roman governor Quintianus pursued for her wealth and beauty; refused, he had her breasts torn off and at last killed her in prison — the apostle Peter appeared to heal her in her cell.

02-06.title: St. Photius the Great, Patriarch of Constantinople; St. Bucolus of Smyrna
02-06.summary: Photius, the great ninth-century patriarch of vast learning who defended Orthodoxy against the Filioque and confirmed the mission to the Slavs. Also commemorated: Bucolus, the disciple of John the Theologian and second bishop of Smyrna.

02-07.title: St. Parthenius, Bishop of Lampsacus
02-07.summary: A fourth-century bishop of the Hellespont known for his power over demons, whom he cast out from a young madman at the bidding of Constantine the Great.

02-08.title: Great-martyr Theodore the General (Stratelates)
02-08.summary: A fourth-century officer of Heraclea on the Black Sea who under Licinius openly broke the city's idols, was crucified, pierced through with arrows, and beheaded — the elder of the two great Theodores.

02-09.title: Martyr Nicephorus of Antioch
02-09.summary: A layman of Antioch who, having quarreled grievously with the presbyter Sapricius and afterwards humbled himself even to lying at his feet, took up the martyr's crown that Sapricius had spurned.

02-10.title: Hieromartyr Charalampias the Wonderworker, Bishop of Magnesia
02-10.summary: A bishop of second-century Magnesia in Thessaly who at one hundred and thirteen years old endured many torments under Septimius Severus and converted his judges before being beheaded.

02-11.title: Hieromartyr Blaise, Bishop of Sebaste in Armenia
02-11.summary: A fourth-century bishop and physician who fled Diocletian's persecution into a cave where wild animals came to him for healing; arrested and tortured with wool-combs, he was finally beheaded.

02-12.title: St. Meletius, Archbishop of Antioch; St. Alexis, Metropolitan of Moscow
02-12.summary: Meletius of Antioch (4th c.), who at his very enthronement preached against the Arians and was sent into three exiles. Alexis of Moscow (14th c.), bishop and statesman, healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian church together in dark days.

also.name: St. Alexis, Metropolitan of Moscow
also.summary: Fourteenth-century hierarch who healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian Church together.

02-13.title: St. Martinianus the Hermit
02-13.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Caesarea in Palestine who burned himself slightly to drive out the temptation a wandering woman had set in him, and fled to a deserted island where he reposed in peace.

02-14.title: St. Cyril, Equal-to-the-Apostles, brother of Methodius
02-14.summary: The younger of the brothers from Thessalonica, whose love of learning earned him the surname 'the Philosopher' and whose translations gave the Slavs their Scriptures. He reposed at Rome at forty-two.

02-15.title: Apostle Onesimus of the Seventy
02-15.summary: The runaway slave of Philemon at Colossae whom Paul met in his Roman imprisonment and sent back to his master as a brother; later bishop of Ephesus after Timothy, and crowned with martyrdom at Rome.

02-16.title: Martyrs Pamphilus of Caesarea and his twelve companions
02-16.summary: A presbyter of Caesarea in Palestine and pupil of Origen who established the great library there, and was beheaded with eleven others under Maximinus Daia after long imprisonment.

02-17.title: Great-martyr Theodore the Tyro (the Recruit)
02-17.summary: A young recruit of the Roman army at Amasea under Maximian who burned down the temple of the Mother of the Gods and was burned alive himself; commemorated also on the Saturday of the First Week of Lent for the miracle of the boiled wheat.

02-18.title: St. Leo the Great, Pope of Rome
02-18.summary: The fifth-century bishop of Rome whose Tome to Flavian became the dogmatic foundation of the Fourth Ecumenical Council at Chalcedon: two natures, perfect God and perfect man, in one Christ.

02-19.title: Apostle Archippus of the Seventy
02-19.summary: Disciple of Paul named in his letter to Philemon ('our fellow soldier'), bishop of Colossae after Epaphras, who there worked through the earthquakes that twice destroyed the city's church.

02-20.title: St. Leo, Bishop of Catania in Sicily
02-20.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Catania remembered for his miracle of binding the sorcerer Heliodorus, whom he led to his own execution in the flames at the bath-house and was preserved untouched.

02-21.title: St. Timothy of Symbola
02-21.summary: A sixth-century monk of Bithynia who as a young man was a disciple of the elder Theoctistus and afterwards became himself an abbot, founding the monastery of Symbola at the foot of Mount Olympus.

02-22.title: Finding of the Relics of the Holy Martyrs at the Gate of Eugenius
02-22.summary: In the reign of Arcadius the bones of unknown martyrs of earlier persecutions were uncovered near the Eugenian Gate of Constantinople; one of the martyrs appeared to declare himself the apostle Andronicus and his companion Junia.

02-23.title: Hieromartyr Polycarp, Bishop of Smyrna
02-23.summary: The disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Smyrna for sixty-five years, who under Marcus Aurelius answered the proconsul's demand to revile Christ: 'Eighty and six years have I served Him, and He has done me no wrong; how then can I blaspheme my King who saved me?'

02-24.title: First and Second Findings of the Honorable Head of John the Baptist
02-24.summary: The head of the Forerunner, hidden by Herodias in a clay vessel and lost, was rediscovered first by two pilgrims of Edessa near Sebaste, and a second time during the iconoclasm at Emesa.

02-25.title: St. Tarasius, Archbishop of Constantinople
02-25.summary: A ninth-century patriarch who under the Empress Irene presided over the Seventh Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, which restored the holy icons to the Church.

02-26.title: St. Porphyrius, Bishop of Gaza
02-26.summary: A fifth-century bishop who through the long-prayed labor of years uprooted the entrenched paganism of Gaza, bringing down the great temple of Marnas by imperial decree after a famine breaks at his intercession.

02-27.title: St. Procopius the Confessor of Decapolis
02-27.summary: An eighth-century monk of Asia Minor and defender of the holy icons under Leo the Isaurian, beaten in prison and exiled with his disciple Basil until the death of the emperor restored him.

02-28.title: St. Basil the Confessor, disciple of St. Procopius
02-28.summary: The fellow sufferer and disciple of Procopius of Decapolis, who endured the same prisons and scourging for the icons and lived to teach in freedom after the great persecution had subsided.

03-01.title: Virgin-martyr Eudokia of Heliopolis
03-01.summary: A Samaritan woman of Heliopolis in Phoenicia, immensely wealthy and famously beautiful, who at the witness of a Christian monk gave away all her possessions and entered a convent — beheaded for the faith under Trajan.

03-02.title: Hieromartyr Theodotus, Bishop of Cyrene
03-02.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Cyrenaica under Licinius who, scourged and racked, was finally beheaded after his judge had himself converted at the spectacle of his patience.

03-03.title: Martyrs Eutropius, Cleonicus, and Basiliscus
03-03.summary: Three companions in arms of the great-martyr Theodore the Tyro who, after his crowning, were arrested at Amasea and crucified together for the same confession.

03-04.title: St. Gerasimus of the Jordan
03-04.summary: A fifth-century desert father of the Jordan valley near Jericho who is the saint of the lion — he drew the thorn from a wild lion's paw and the beast became his servant until his death.

03-05.title: Martyr Conon the Gardener of Isauria
03-05.summary: A simple gardener of Isauria under Decius, baptized by Michael the Archangel and known for his quiet boldness; he was burned alive in a fire he calmly walked into himself.

03-06.title: The Forty-two Martyrs of Amorion
03-06.summary: Forty-two officers of Byzantine Amorion taken to Samarra in 845 after the city's fall to the Caliph Mu'tasim; after seven years of imprisonment they were beheaded together for refusing Islam.

03-07.title: The Hieromartyrs of Cherson (Basil, Ephraim, Eugenius, Capito, Agathodorus, Etherius, Elpidius)
03-07.summary: Seven bishops sent from Jerusalem in the time of Diocletian to evangelize the Crimea who, in the fierce paganism of Cherson, were one by one stoned, dragged behind chariots, or beheaded.

03-08.title: St. Theophylact the Confessor, Bishop of Nicomedia
03-08.summary: A ninth-century friend of Tarasius of Constantinople and bishop of Nicomedia who under Leo the Armenian was driven from his see to thirty years of exile in a fortress on the Bosphorus for the icons.

03-09.title: The Forty Holy Martyrs of Sebaste
03-09.summary: Forty Roman soldiers of the legion at Sebaste in Armenia under Licinius who, refusing the imperial sacrifice, were left naked on a frozen lake. One forsook them for the warm bath at the shore; one of the guards leapt in to take his place, and the forty crowns remained intact.

03-10.title: Martyr Quadratus and his companions at Corinth
03-10.summary: Five Christians of Corinth under Decius — Quadratus chief among them — born in a forest where their mothers had hidden during the persecution, and beheaded together when they came of age to confess.

03-11.title: St. Sophronius, Patriarch of Jerusalem
03-11.summary: The seventh-century hierarch who at the surrender of Jerusalem to the Caliph Umar received from him the covenant protecting the holy places, and lived just long enough to grieve the loss before his repose.

03-12.title: St. Theophanes the Confessor of Sigriane
03-12.summary: A ninth-century chronicler-monk of Bithynia whose Chronographia preserves the events of his age, and who under Leo the Armenian was beaten and exiled to Samothrace for the holy icons, dying there of his torments.

03-13.title: Translation of the Relics of St. Nicephorus, Patriarch of Constantinople
03-13.summary: Nineteen years after his death in exile, the bones of the confessor Nicephorus were carried in triumph from his island monastery back to the city he had ruled before iconoclasm drove him out.

03-14.title: St. Benedict of Nursia
03-14.summary: The sixth-century father of Western monasticism whose Rule for the cenobium of Monte Cassino joined the desert's wisdom to the moderate work of community — 'pray and work,' in everything seeking the love of Christ.

03-15.title: Martyr Agapius and his seven companions at Caesarea
03-15.summary: Eight Christians of Caesarea in Palestine under Galerius Maximianus, who at separate audiences before the governor each refused to sacrifice and were given to the beasts together in the arena.

03-16.title: Martyr Sabinus of Egypt
03-16.summary: A nobleman of fourth-century Hermopolis under Diocletian who from a hidden cell in the marshes was betrayed by a beggar to whom he had given alms, and was drowned in the Nile for confessing Christ.

03-17.title: St. Alexis the Man of God
03-17.summary: A fifth-century noble of Rome who on his wedding day fled to Edessa and lived seventeen years as a beggar at a church door, then returned home unrecognized to spend another seventeen years as the family's anonymous servant under his father's stairs.

03-18.title: St. Cyril, Archbishop of Jerusalem
03-18.summary: The fourth-century catechist of the newly baptized whose lectures from the Anastasis preserve the rites and teaching of the Jerusalem church; exiled three times by Arian opposition, restored by the Second Ecumenical Council.

03-19.title: Martyrs Chrysanthus and Daria
03-19.summary: A young convert of Roman Alexandria and the Vestal virgin sent to dissuade him; instead she became his wife in faith and they lived in chastity together. Both were buried alive in a sand-pit under Numerian.

03-20.title: The Holy Fathers slain at the monastery of St. Sabbas
03-20.summary: Forty-four monks of the Great Lavra of St. Sabbas martyred in 614 by a band of Saracens — burned in their cells or driven into a cave where their bones were left in heaps.

03-21.title: St. James the Confessor, Bishop of Catania
03-21.summary: A ninth-century monk of the Studion monastery in Constantinople, raised to the see of Catania in Sicily, who endured beating, imprisonment, and exile under Leo the Armenian for the holy icons.

03-22.title: Hieromartyr Basil, Presbyter of Ancyra
03-22.summary: A fourth-century priest of Ancyra under Julian the Apostate who openly broke the imperial idols and was tortured slowly through seven days of progressively crueler ordeals before his beheading.

03-23.title: Martyr Nicon of Sicily and his hundred and ninety-nine disciples
03-23.summary: A second-century convert from a centurion's household in Naples who fled to Sicily, gathered 199 fellow soldiers as monks, and was beheaded with them all under the persecution of Decius.

03-24.title: Forefeast of the Annunciation; St. Artemon
03-24.summary: The eve of the Annunciation. Also commemorated: Artemon, presbyter of Laodicea in the second century, ordained by Paul on his last journey through Phrygia and afterwards bishop in his old age.

03-25.title: The Annunciation to Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
03-25.summary: Today is the beginning of our salvation, and the revelation of the eternal mystery. The Son of God becomes the Son of the Virgin, and Gabriel proclaims the good tidings of grace.

03-26.title: The Synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel
03-26.summary: The day after the Annunciation honors the one who first declared the mystery — Gabriel, who told Zacharias of John, and the Virgin of her Son, and now is called the announcing Archangel of the great counsel of God.

03-27.title: Martyr Matrona of Thessalonica
03-27.summary: A Christian slave girl of Thessalonica under Maximian whose Jewish mistress, finding her at prayer in secret, beat her to death over the course of three days.

03-28.title: St. Hilarion the New, abbot of Pelecete
03-28.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelecete on the Black Sea, defender of the icons under Leo the Isaurian and his successors, who repeatedly endured imprisonment and torture for the cause.

03-29.title: Hieromartyrs Mark of Arethusa and Cyril the Deacon
03-29.summary: A bishop in Syria under Julian the Apostate who in his youth had broken a pagan temple, and the deacon Cyril of Heliopolis; both were tortured by their enraged neighbors when Julian came to power, neither yielding to the smear of incense.

03-30.title: St. John Climacus, author of The Ladder of Divine Ascent
03-30.summary: The seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose Ladder maps the soul's ascent to God in thirty steps — the work commemorated on the fourth Sunday of Great Lent and read in monasteries throughout the season.

03-31.title: Hieromartyr Hypatius, Bishop of Gangra
03-31.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Paphlagonia who took part in the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, and on his return journey was set upon by Novatian heretics and stoned to death.

04-01.title: St. Mary of Egypt
04-01.summary: The fifth-century desert ascetic whose long penitence in the wilderness beyond the Jordan is the icon of Lenten transformation; she lived forty-seven years unseen until the priest Zosimas found her, gave her communion, and buried her.

04-02.title: St. Titus the Wonderworker
04-02.summary: A monk of the eighth century at a monastery near the Hellespont, known both for the gift of healing and for the meek silence that healed many more than his miracles did.

04-03.title: St. Nicetas the Confessor, abbot of Medikion
04-03.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Medikion in Bithynia, scourged and exiled to a fortified rock under Leo the Armenian for confessing the holy icons.

04-04.title: St. Joseph the Hymnographer
04-04.summary: The ninth-century monk of Sicily, exiled to Constantinople by the Arab conquest, whose hymns are appointed in every canon of the Octoechos and whose canons fill the Menaion through every saint's day.

04-05.title: Martyrs Theodulus the Reader and Agathopodes the Deacon
04-05.summary: A young reader and an aged deacon of fourth-century Thessalonica under Diocletian, bound together back-to-back and cast into the sea, where their bodies came ashore intact for burial.

04-06.title: St. Eutychius, Archbishop of Constantinople
04-06.summary: A sixth-century patriarch who presided over the Fifth Ecumenical Council at Constantinople, was deposed and exiled by Justinian for theological reasons, and was triumphantly restored under Justin II.

04-07.title: St. George the Confessor, Metropolitan of Mytilene
04-07.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Lesbos who under the iconoclast Leo the Armenian endured beatings and exile rather than surrender the icons of the Church.

04-08.title: Apostles Herodion, Agabus, Rufus, Asyncritus, Phlegon, and Hermes
04-08.summary: Six of the Seventy, all named in Romans 16: Herodion bishop of Patras and kinsman of Paul; Agabus the prophet of the famine; Rufus of Thebes; Asyncritus of Hyrcania; Phlegon of Marathon; Hermes of Philippopolis.

04-09.title: Martyr Eupsychius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
04-09.summary: A newly-married noble of Caesarea under Julian the Apostate who together with his bridal companions broke down the temple of Fortune; for this he was beheaded, and a famine which followed the deed lifted only at the emperor's death.

04-10.title: Martyrs Terence, Pompeius, Africanus, Maximus, Zenon, Alexander, and Theodore of Africa
04-10.summary: Seven Christians of Carthage in the third century under Decius who, refusing in turn each form of induced sacrifice, were beheaded together in the city's amphitheater.

04-11.title: Hieromartyr Antipas of Pergamum
04-11.summary: Disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Pergamum mentioned by name in the Revelation as 'My faithful witness'; he was roasted alive in a brass bull-shaped idol under Domitian.

04-12.title: St. Basil the Confessor, Bishop of Parium
04-12.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Parium on the Hellespont who refused to sign the iconoclast decree of the council of Hieria and was beaten and exiled, surviving to die in peace.

04-13.title: Hieromartyr Artemon, Presbyter of Laodicea in Pisidia
04-13.summary: A second-century priest of Pisidia under Diocletian, an aged man of eighty-nine, who having destroyed pagan idols was given to two ravenous lions and instead tamed them.

04-14.title: St. Martin the Confessor, Pope of Rome
04-14.summary: The seventh-century bishop of Rome who at the Lateran council condemned the heresy of monothelitism and was for this seized by imperial troops, brought to Constantinople, tried, and exiled to Cherson where he died of starvation.

04-15.title: Apostles Aristarchus, Pudens, and Trophimus of the Seventy
04-15.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul in his letters: Aristarchus the Macedonian of Thessalonica who shared Paul's Roman imprisonment, Pudens the Roman senator who hosted Paul at his house, and Trophimus the Ephesian who fell ill at Miletus.

04-16.title: Virgin-martyrs Agape, Irene, and Chionia of Aquileia
04-16.summary: Three sisters of Aquileia under Diocletian who in their parents' country house had hidden away copies of the Scriptures; on their refusal to surrender them, the elder two were burned and the third pierced with arrows.

04-17.title: Hieromartyr Symeon of Persia, with the martyrs of Persia
04-17.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Seleucia and Ctesiphon under the Persian Sapor II, beheaded with a hundred and fifty fellow Christians the day after Pascha for refusing to gather tax from the Christians or worship the sun.

04-18.title: St. John, disciple of St. Gregory the Decapolite
04-18.summary: A ninth-century disciple of Gregory of Decapolis at Constantinople, an unyielding confessor of the icons under the second iconoclasm, who reposed in peace at the monastery his teacher had founded.

04-19.title: St. John of the Ancient Caves
04-19.summary: A monk of the lavra of St. Chariton in Palestine in the eighth century, called of the Ancient Caves because the place he settled had been used by hermits before him going back to the third century.

04-20.title: St. Theodore Trichinas
04-20.summary: A fifth-century recluse of Constantinople who from his cell at Asomatos lived in such severe asceticism that his hair-shirt (whence his name) wore against his skin for forty years; many miracles followed at his shrine.

04-21.title: Hieromartyr Januarius of Beneventum and his companions
04-21.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Beneventum in Italy under Diocletian who with six others was beheaded at Pozzuoli; the cooled blood preserved in a vial at Naples has been observed to liquefy at its annual exposition.

04-22.title: St. Theodore the Sykeote, Bishop of Anastasiopolis
04-22.summary: A sixth-century Galatian who from boyhood took up the monastic life, became a great wonderworker, was made bishop against his will, and reposed back in the small cave-cell that had been his first hermitage.

04-23.title: Great-martyr George the Trophy-bearer
04-23.summary: The young Cappadocian officer of the Roman army under Diocletian whose endurance through every torture and final beheading made him the icon of the soldier-saint of all the East; his cult spread from Lydda throughout the world.

04-24.title: Martyr Sabbas the Stratelates and seventy soldiers with him
04-24.summary: A Gothic officer in the Roman army at Rome under Aurelian who together with seventy of his fellow soldiers was beheaded in 272 for confessing Christ.

04-25.title: Holy Apostle and Evangelist Mark
04-25.summary: The companion of Peter who wrote the second Gospel from the apostle's preaching at Rome, and the first bishop of Alexandria — dragged through the streets of the city by a rope until he gave up his soul.

04-26.title: Hieromartyr Basil, Bishop of Amasea
04-26.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Amasea on the Black Sea who tried to deliver the empress Glaphyra from the unwanted attentions of Licinius; the emperor in his rage had Basil beheaded on the shore of the Bosphorus.

04-27.title: Hieromartyr Symeon, kinsman of the Lord, second Bishop of Jerusalem
04-27.summary: Son of Clopas the brother of Joseph the Betrothed, who succeeded James as bishop of Jerusalem after his martyrdom. He was crucified under Trajan at the age of one hundred and twenty.

04-28.title: Apostles Jason and Sosipater of the Seventy
04-28.summary: Two kinsmen of Paul named in Romans 16, who together evangelized the island of Corfu; their preaching converted a number of brigands at the king's prison, and they reposed in peace after long labors.

04-29.title: The Nine Martyrs of Cyzicus
04-29.summary: Nine Christians from various cities of Asia Minor who, gathered at Cyzicus on the Sea of Marmara, were beheaded together for refusing the imperial sacrifice in the fourth century.

04-30.title: Holy Apostle James, brother of John the Theologian
04-30.summary: One of the sons of Zebedee, who left his boats with his brother at the Lord's call and stood with him on Tabor; the first apostle to be martyred, slain by Herod at Jerusalem in the year 44.
